topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT604 - AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Static and Dynamic Load Capacity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Static Load Capacity (C₀) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Static Load Rating (C₀):** The static load rating is defined as the load acting on a non-rotating, slowly oscillating, or slowly rotating bearing, at which the permanent deformation of the rolling element (ball or roller) at the most heavily loaded contact point reaches **0.0001 of the rolling element diameter (d)**. This threshold is universally accepted by **ISO 76:2006** and **IS 6455** standards, and forms the basis for all static capacity calculations in KTU board examinations.

**Units:** The static load capacity is expressed in **kilonewtons (kN)** in SI practice, or in **Newton (N)** for small precision bearings.

## 1.2 Dynamic Load Capacity (C) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dynamic Load Rating (C):** The dynamic load rating is the constant, steady load which a rolling bearing can theoretically endure for a rating life of **1 million revolutions (10⁶ revolutions)** before the first evidence of fatigue failure (pitting/spalling) develops on the raceway. This is governed by the **ISO 281:2007** and **IS 6455** standards.

**Units:** Expressed in **kilonewtons (kN)**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are stacking **identical iron bricks** on top of a wooden plank:

- **Static Load Capacity (C₀)** — Think of placing a heavy object on a **stationary** table. The table doesn't break from fatigue; it either survives the weight (elastic recovery) or deforms permanently (dents in the wood). The maximum weight the table can bear before it visibly **dents** is its static capacity.

- **Dynamic Load Capacity (C)** — Now imagine a **swinging bridge** that opens and closes 50 times a day. Even though each cycle applies a moderate load (well within the static capacity), after **1 million open-close cycles**, the metal fatigues and cracks. The load that gives exactly 1 million cycles of life before fatigue is the dynamic capacity.

In automotive engineering:
- **Static (C₀)** governs **parked, sudden shock, or reversing loads** — e.g., vehicle jacked up, brake caliper loads, crash loads.
- **Dynamic (C)** governs **continuous running loads** — e.g., wheel bearings at highway speeds, transmission gears, crankshaft journals.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners often ask "Why is C₀ higher than C?" Answer: Because fatigue (C) limits life much earlier than plastic indentation (C₀). A bearing can carry 3–5× more load statically than it can dynamically for the same life.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit | Source |
|-----------|--------|-----------------------|--------|
| Fatigue Life Constant | e | **3** for ball bearings, **10/3** for roller bearings | ISO 281 |
| Indentation Factor | f₀ | **0** to **50** (depending on contact geometry) | ISO 76 |
| Reference Life (dynamic) | L₁₀ | **1,000,000 revolutions** | ISO 281 |
| Static Safety Factor | S₀ | **1.0 to 4.0** (depending on application) | IS 6455 |
| EHL Film Parameter | κ | **1 to 2** | Hamrock-Dowson |
| Hertzian Contact Pressure Limit | σ_lim | **4000 MPa** (ball) / **4200 MPa** (roller) | ISO/TR 1281 |

---

## 1.5 Fundamental Relationship Between C and C₀

The ratio **C / C₀** for a given bearing series is a fixed geometric property:

$$\frac{C}{C_0} \approx 1.5 \text{ to } 2.0 \quad \text{(for ball bearings)}$$

$$\frac{C}{C_0} \approx 2.0 \text{ to } 3.0 \quad \text{(for roller bearings)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The dynamic capacity is **always numerically smaller** than the static capacity for the same bearing, because dynamic failure (fatigue) precedes static failure (indentation) under repetitive loading.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why Two Capacities Exist

Rolling element bearings experience two distinct failure modes:

1. **Fatigue Failure (Dynamic)**: Progressive subsurface cracks initiate below the raceway surface due to repeated Hertzian shear stress cycling. Cracks propagate to the surface and produce **pitting** or **spalling**.

2. **Plastic Indentation (Static)**: When the load is so high (or rotation so slow) that fatigue does not have time to occur, the asperities permanently deform at the contact patch, leaving **brinelling** marks.

The bearing industry chose **two separate rating numbers** so that designers can apply two independent safety criteria.

## 2.2 Rating Life Equation (ISO 281)

The most fundamental equation in bearing design is the **basic rating life** (also called **L₁₀ life**, since 90% of bearings will exceed this life):

$$L_{10} = \left( \frac{C}{P} \right)^p \quad \text{[million revolutions]}$$

Where:
- **L₁₀** = basic rating life in millions of revolutions
- **C** = dynamic load rating (kN)
- **P** = equivalent dynamic load (kN)
- **p** = exponent of the life equation = **3** for ball bearings, **10/3** for roller bearings

### 2.2.1 Conversion of L₁₀ to Hours

$$L_{10h} = \frac{10^6 \cdot L_{10}}{60 \cdot n} = \frac{16667 \cdot L_{10}}{n} \quad \text{[hours]}$$

Where **n** = rotational speed in **rpm**.

### 2.2.2 Modified Rating Life (ISO 281:2007)

Modern ISO standards include **lubrication cleanliness** and **a₁, a_ISO** factors:

$$L_{10m} = a_1 \cdot a_{ISO} \cdot L_{10} = a_1 \cdot a_{ISO} \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p$$

Where:
- **a₁** = reliability factor (e.g., a₁ = **1.00** for 90% reliability, **0.62** for 99%)
- **a_ISO** = combined factor for lubrication, contamination, and operating conditions

## 2.3 Equivalent Dynamic Load (P)

When a bearing carries both radial (Fr) and axial (Fa) loads simultaneously, we compute an **equivalent load** that produces the same fatigue life:

$$P = X \cdot F_r + Y \cdot F_a \quad \text{[kN]}$$

Where:
- **X** = radial factor
- **Y** = axial factor
- These values depend on the **ratio Fa/Fr** and the bearing geometry; they are tabulated in SKF, FAG, and NTN catalogues.

For **pure radial loading** (Fa = 0): **P = Fr**.

## 2.4 Equivalent Static Load (P₀)

The static equivalent load is the static load that would produce the same permanent deformation as the actual combined static load:

$$P_0 = X_0 \cdot F_r + Y_0 \cdot F_a \quad \text{[kN]}$$

Where **X₀** and **Y₀** are the static radial and axial factors from the manufacturer catalogue.

## 2.5 Static Safety Factor Check

After computing P₀, the bearing is acceptable if:

$$S_0 = \frac{C_0}{P_0} \geq S_{0,min}$$

| Application | S₀,min |
|-------------|--------|
| Smooth, vibration-free operation | **1.0** |
| Normal operation (most automotive) | **1.5 – 2.0** |
| Shock loads, gearboxes | **2.0 – 3.0** |
| Crane bearings, crusher bearings | **3.0 – 4.0** |

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — every KTU 2024 numerical question on this topic tests one of these formulas.**

| # | Formula | Use Case | Units |
|---|---------|----------|-------|
| 1 | $L_{10} = (C/P)^p$ | Basic rating life | Million revs |
| 2 | $L_{10h} = 16667 \cdot L_{10} / n$ | Convert to hours | hours |
| 3 | $P = X F_r + Y F_a$ | Equivalent dynamic load | kN |
| 4 | $P_0 = X_0 F_r + Y_0 F_a$ | Equivalent static load | kN |
| 5 | $S_0 = C_0 / P_0$ | Static safety factor | dimensionless |
| 6 | $L_{10m} = a_1 a_{ISO} (C/P)^p$ | Modified rating life | Million revs |
| 7 | $C_{req} = P \cdot L_{10}^{1/p}$ | Required dynamic capacity | kN |
| 8 | $\sigma_{max} = (3F / (2\pi a b))$ | Hertzian contact pressure | MPa |
| 9 | $Q / Q_{max} = \cos(\psi)^{1/2}$ | Load distribution on rolling elements | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\delta / \delta_{max} = (1 - 1/\epsilon)\cos(\psi)$ | Elastic deflection (Stribeck) | dimensionless |

## 2.7 Real-World Utility in Automotive Engineering

- **Wheel hub bearings**: Selected primarily by **dynamic capacity** because they rotate continuously at vehicle speed. A typical passenger car wheel bearing operates at ~1000 rpm at 100 km/h.
- **Clutch release bearings**: Selected primarily by **static capacity** because they are loaded only momentarily during gear shifts and remain stationary otherwise.
- **Transmission output shaft bearings**: Both criteria are checked; the **dynamic life** governs, but **static safety** is checked against gear-shock loads.
- **Steering column bearings**: Static-dominant, but with low S₀ values acceptable because loads are well-defined and impact-free.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Note:** Tesla Model 3 rear motor bearings operate at ~17,000 rpm, requiring careful use of **modified L₁₀m** with a_ISO > 0.8 for EHL lubricant regimes. This is why automotive OEMs use **synthetic PAO oils with extreme pressure (EP) additives**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Lundberg-Palmgren Life Equation

This is the foundational analytical work behind the **dynamic load rating (C)** used in all modern catalogues.

### 3.1.1 Starting Postulate

For a rolling element subjected to cyclic Hertzian shear stress τ₀ at depth z₀ below the contact surface, the probability of survival after N stress cycles is:

$$S(N) = \exp\left(-\frac{N^a \tau_0^a V}{z_0^h}\right)$$

Where:
- **V** = stressed volume
- **a** = Weibull slope (typically **9/8 for ball** and **10/9 for roller**)
- **h** = depth exponent (≈ **31/3**)

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1** — Integrate survival probability over the **raceway** (load distribution varies around the raceway from Q_max to 0).

Setting survival probability = 0.9 (i.e., 10% failure for L₁₀ life):

$$0.1 = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_0^{\pi} \exp\left(-\frac{N^a \tau_0^a V_0}{z_0^h}\right) \cos(\psi)\, d\psi$$

Where the **load-deflection relationship** (Stribeck) is:

$$Q = K \delta^{3/2}$$

**Step 2** — Express τ₀ in terms of Q:

From Hertz contact theory for point contact (ball-on-raceway):

$$\tau_0 = \tau_{max} \cdot f(Q) \propto Q^{2/3}$$

**Step 3** — Substitute and integrate. After laborious algebra (Lundberg & Palmgren, 1947):

$$L_{10} = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p \quad \text{with} \quad p = 3 \text{ (ball)}, \quad p = 10/3 \text{ (roller)}$$

**Step 4** — Definition of C (dynamic load rating):

By definition, when **P = C**, **L₁₀ = 1 million revolutions**. This convention **fixes** the numerical value of C as the load corresponding to L₁₀ = 10⁶.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example (KTU Board Style — 14 Mark Problem Setup)

> **Problem Statement [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]:**
> A deep groove ball bearing 6308 is subjected to a radial load Fr = 5 kN and an axial load Fa = 2 kN at a speed of n = 1450 rpm. The bearing is required to give 5000 hours of life with 90% reliability. From the manufacturer's catalogue:
> - C = **41 kN**, C₀ = **24 kN**
> - X = 0.56, Y = 1.15 (for Fa/Fr > e = 0.26)
> - X₀ = 0.6, Y₀ = 0.5
> 
> Determine: (a) The actual rating life in hours; (b) Whether the bearing is statically safe for S₀,min = 1.5.

### 3.2.1 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the load ratio and select X, Y**

$$\frac{F_a}{F_r} = \frac{2}{5} = 0.4$$

Check whether this exceeds **e**:

$$F_a / F_r = 0.4 > e = 0.26 \implies \text{Use } X = 0.56, \; Y = 1.15$$

**Step 2 — Compute the equivalent dynamic load P**

$$P = X F_r + Y F_a$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= (0.56)(5) + (1.15)(2) \\
  &= 2.80 + 2.30 \\
  &= 5.10 \; \text{kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the basic rating life L₁₀ (million revs)**

For a **ball bearing**, p = 3:

$$L_{10} = \left(\frac{C}{P}\right)^p = \left(\frac{41}{5.10}\right)^3$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{C}{P} &= \frac{41}{5.10} = 8.039 \\
L_{10} &= (8.039)^3 \\
       &= 519.5 \; \text{million revolutions}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to hours**

$$L_{10h} = \frac{16667 \times L_{10}}{n} = \frac{16667 \times 519.5}{1450}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{10h} &= \frac{8{,}657{,}837}{1450} \\
        &= 5971 \; \text{hours}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Verify against the requirement**

$$L_{10h,actual} = 5971 \; \text{hours} > L_{10h,required} = 5000 \; \text{hours}$$

✅ **The bearing is dynamically adequate.**

**Step 6 — Compute the equivalent static load P₀**

$$P_0 = X_0 F_r + Y_0 F_a$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P_0 &= (0.6)(5) + (0.5)(2) \\
    &= 3.0 + 1.0 \\
    &= 4.0 \; \text{kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Compute the static safety factor S₀**

$$S_0 = \frac{C_0}{P_0} = \frac{24}{4.0} = 6.0$$

**Step 8 — Compare against the minimum**

$$S_0 = 6.0 > S_{0,min} = 1.5$$

✅ **The bearing is statically safe — actually over-designed for static load.**

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Failure Mode vs. Load Type

| Loading Condition | Operating State | Governing Capacity | KTU Equation |
|-------------------|-----------------|--------------------|--------------|
| Bearing stationary, heavy load | At rest / parked | **Static (C₀)** | $S_0 = C_0 / P_0 \geq S_{0,min}$ |
| Bearing rotating, constant load | Continuous duty | **Dynamic (C)** | $L_{10} = (C/P)^p$ |
| Bearing rotating, variable load | Automotive duty cycle | **Dynamic (C)** with spectrum factor | $L_{10} = (C/P_{eq})^p$ |
| Sudden shock, no rotation | Crash / impact | **Static (C₀)** | Use **f₀** contact factor |
| Slow oscillation | Steering / slewing | **Static (C₀)** | $S_0 \geq 2.0$ recommended |

## 3.4 Derivation of Equivalent Load Spectrum (Cumulative)

For automotive duty cycles with **N₁ cycles at load P₁**, **N₂ at P₂**, ..., the **equivalent constant load** is:

$$P_{eq} = \left(\frac{N_1 P_1^p + N_2 P_2^p + \cdots + N_k P_k^p}{\sum N_i}\right)^{1/p}$$

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. Each load Pi contributes $N_i P_i^p$ damage (Miner's rule).
2. Total damage = sum of all $N_i P_i^p$.
3. An equivalent constant load P_eq over the same total cycles gives the same damage:

$$P_{eq}^p \sum N_i = \sum N_i P_i^p$$

$$P_{eq} = \left(\frac{\sum N_i P_i^p}{\sum N_i}\right)^{1/p}$$

Then **L₁₀ = (C / P_eq)^p**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Bearing Capacity Selection Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Application] --> B{Operating Mode?}
    B -->|Rotating| C[Compute Dynamic Life L10]
    B -->|Stationary| D[Compute Static Safety S0]
    B -->|Both| E[Run BOTH Checks]
    C --> C1[Compute Equivalent Dynamic Load P]
    C1 --> C2[Apply L10 = C by P cubed p]
    C2 --> C3{L10h exceeds Required?}
    C3 -->|Yes| F[Select Bearing]
    C3 -->|No| G[Upsize Bearing Series]
    D --> D1[Compute Equivalent Static Load P0]
    D1 --> D2[Apply S0 = C0 by P0]
    D2 --> D3{S0 exceeds S0min?}
    D3 -->|Yes| F
    D3 -->|No| G
    E --> E1[Dynamic Check]
    E --> E2[Static Check]
    E1 --> F
    E2 --> F
    G --> C
    F --> H[Verify Lubrication aISO]
    H --> I[Final Selection]
```

## 4.2 Load Distribution on Rolling Elements (Stribeck Curve)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Outer Raceway] --> B[Load Zone = 180 deg]
    B --> C[Qmax at Bottom]
    B --> D[Q decreases to 0 at Edges]
    C --> E[Load Distribution Q of psi]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Stribeck: Q = Qmax x cos to power 1/2 psi]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Dynamic vs Static Capacity

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT[Input Loads]
        FR[Radial Force Fr]
        FA[Axial Force Fa]
        N[Rotational Speed n rpm]
    end
    subgraph EQUIV[Equivalent Load Calculation]
        PEQ[P = X Fr + Y Fa]
        P0EQ[P0 = X0 Fr + Y0 Fa]
    end
    subgraph CAP[Capacity Domains]
        CDYN[Dynamic Capacity C]
        CSTA[Static Capacity C0]
    end
    subgraph LIFE[Life and Safety]
        LLIFE[L10 = C by P cubed p]
        SSAF[S0 = C0 by P0]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Decision Output]
        DEC1{Dynamic OK?}
        DEC2{Static OK?}
        OUTFIN[Selected Bearing]
    end
    FR --> PEQ
    FA --> PEQ
    FR --> P0EQ
    FA --> P0EQ
    PEQ --> LLIFE
    P0EQ --> SSAF
    CDYN --> LLIFE
    CSTA --> SSAF
    N --> LLIFE
    LLIFE --> DEC1
    SSAF --> DEC2
    DEC1 -->|Yes| OUTFIN
    DEC2 -->|Yes| OUTFIN
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Safeguard Verification

All node IDs are alphanumeric (PEQ, P0EQ, CDYN, CSTA, LLIFE, SSAF, DEC1, DEC2, OUTFIN, etc.) and contain **no reserved keywords** (end, subgraph, graph, style). All labels use **clean uppercase text** with no markdown formatting tags inside double quotes.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: Define dynamic load capacity of a rolling contact bearing and state the standard life used to define it.**

**Model Answer:**
The dynamic load capacity (C) of a rolling contact bearing is defined as the constant radial load which a bearing can theoretically withstand for a **rating life of 1 million revolutions (10⁶ revs)** before the first evidence of material fatigue (pitting) develops on the raceway. This definition is standardized in **ISO 281:2007** and **IS 6455**. It is used to compute the rating life using the equation $L_{10} = (C/P)^p$, where p = 3 for ball bearings and 10/3 for roller bearings.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: Distinguish between static load capacity (C₀) and dynamic load capacity (C).**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Static Load Capacity (C₀) | Dynamic Load Capacity (C) |
|-----------|---------------------------|---------------------------|
| Definition | Load that produces 0.0001 d permanent indentation | Load for 10⁶ rev rating life |
| Failure mode | Plastic deformation / brinelling | Material fatigue / pitting |
| Application | Stationary, slow oscillation | Continuous rotation |
| Standard | ISO 76:2006 | ISO 281:2007 |
| Numerical value | Higher (1.5–3× C) | Lower |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

A 6310 deep groove ball bearing in an automotive gearbox is subjected to:
- Radial load **Fr = 8 kN**
- Axial load **Fa = 3 kN**
- Speed **n = 1000 rpm**
- Required life **L₁₀h = 8000 hours** at 90% reliability

From the catalogue:
- C = **62 kN**, C₀ = **38 kN**
- X = 0.56, Y = 1.15 (when Fa/Fr > e = 0.28)
- X₀ = 0.6, Y₀ = 0.5

**Solve:**
**(a)** Compute the equivalent dynamic load P and verify the bearing's life in hours.
**(b)** Compute the equivalent static load P₀ and check whether S₀ ≥ 2.0 is satisfied.

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1:** Compute load ratio:

$$F_a / F_r = 3 / 8 = 0.375 > e = 0.28$$

So use **X = 0.56, Y = 1.15**.

**Step 2:** Equivalent dynamic load:

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= X F_r + Y F_a \\
  &= (0.56)(8) + (1.15)(3) \\
  &= 4.48 + 3.45 \\
  &= 7.93 \; \text{kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Basic rating life (millions of revolutions) with p = 3 for ball:

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{10} &= (C / P)^3 \\
       &= (62 / 7.93)^3 \\
       &= (7.818)^3 \\
       &= 477.8 \; \text{million revs}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Convert to hours:

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{10h} &= 16667 \times L_{10} / n \\
        &= 16667 \times 477.8 / 1000 \\
        &= 7964 \; \text{hours}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5:** Compare:

$$L_{10h} = 7964 \; \text{hours} < L_{10h,required} = 8000 \; \text{hours}$$

❌ **The bearing is marginally under-designed** — select the next larger series (6312).

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1:** Equivalent static load:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_0 &= X_0 F_r + Y_0 F_a \\
    &= (0.6)(8) + (0.5)(3) \\
    &= 4.8 + 1.5 \\
    &= 6.3 \; \text{kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Static safety factor:

$$S_0 = C_0 / P_0 = 38 / 6.3 = 6.03$$

**Step 3:** Compare:

$$S_0 = 6.03 > S_{0,min} = 2.0$$

✅ **The bearing is statically safe.**

**Conclusion:** The bearing is statically safe but dynamically marginal — recommend **upsizing to 6312**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

A cylindrical roller bearing NU 2210 in a transmission countershaft operates under a varying duty cycle:

| Stage | Radial Load (kN) | Duration (revs) |
|-------|------------------|-----------------|
| 1 (urban driving) | 4.5 | 4 × 10⁶ |
| 2 (highway cruise) | 2.0 | 18 × 10⁶ |
| 3 (hill climb) | 7.0 | 3 × 10⁶ |

Speed **n = 2500 rpm**, dynamic capacity **C = 95 kN**, exponent **p = 10/3** (roller bearing). Pure radial loading (Fa = 0).

**Solve:**
**(a)** Compute the equivalent constant load P_eq and the rating life L₁₀ in revolutions and hours.
**(b)** If the application requires **L₁₀h ≥ 10,000 hours**, decide whether the bearing is suitable or recommend a larger series.

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1:** For pure radial loading, **P_i = F_ri**:

$$P_1 = 4.5 \; \text{kN}, \quad P_2 = 2.0 \; \text{kN}, \quad P_3 = 7.0 \; \text{kN}$$

**Step 2:** Equivalent constant load using Miner's rule with p = 10/3:

$$P_{eq} = \left(\frac{\sum N_i P_i^{10/3}}{\sum N_i}\right)^{3/10}$$

**Numerator calculation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
N_1 P_1^{10/3} &= 4 \times 10^6 \times (4.5)^{10/3} \\
              &= 4 \times 10^6 \times 64.43 \\
              &= 257.7 \times 10^6
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
N_2 P_2^{10/3} &= 18 \times 10^6 \times (2.0)^{10/3} \\
              &= 18 \times 10^6 \times 10.08 \\
              &= 181.4 \times 10^6
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
N_3 P_3^{10/3} &= 3 \times 10^6 \times (7.0)^{10/3} \\
              &= 3 \times 10^6 \times 184.2 \\
              &= 552.6 \times 10^6
\end{aligned}$$

$$\sum N_i P_i^{10/3} = 991.7 \times 10^6$$

$$\sum N_i = (4 + 18 + 3) \times 10^6 = 25 \times 10^6$$

**Step 3:** P_eq:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{eq} &= (991.7 / 25)^{3/10} \\
       &= (39.668)^{0.3} \\
       &= 2.99 \; \text{kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Rating life:

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{10} &= (C / P_{eq})^{10/3} \\
       &= (95 / 2.99)^{10/3} \\
       &= (31.77)^{3.333} \\
       &= 31.77^3 \times 31.77^{0.333} \\
       &= 32068 \times 3.158 \\
       &= 101{,}270 \; \text{million revolutions}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5:** Convert to hours:

$$L_{10h} = 16667 \times 101{,}270 / 2500 = 675{,}142 \; \text{hours}$$

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Comparison:**

$$L_{10h} = 675{,}142 \; \text{hours} \gg L_{10h,required} = 10{,}000 \; \text{hours}$$

✅ **The bearing is heavily over-designed.** This is because automotive transmission countershafts typically run at low-to-moderate loads relative to bearing capacity. The current NU 2210 is acceptable, and you could potentially **downsize** to NU 2208 if weight/cost reduction is critical, but only after re-running the calculation with the smaller C value.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Using p = 3 for roller bearings** ❌ — Roller bearings use **p = 10/3 ≈ 3.333**. Using 3 gives a life **3–5% too low**, losing 1 mark on average.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the unit conversion** — Using L₁₀ in revs without converting to hours via **16667/n** loses **2 marks** in the final answer.
>
> 3. **Selecting X, Y based on default values** ❌ — X and Y depend on **Fa/Fr > e or ≤ e**. Always check the ratio against e first.
>
> 4. **Confusing static and dynamic** — Some students compute P and then divide by C₀ instead of using P₀. Remember: **C₀ goes with P₀, C goes with P**.
>
> 5. **Not reporting units** — KTU expects **kN** in final answers, not Newtons. Wrong unit → 0.5 mark deducted.
>
> 6. **Skipping the conclusion statement** — Always end with a clear ✅ or ❌ and a brief engineering recommendation. This is worth **1–2 marks** for "engineering judgment."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This Section Before Exam:**

- **Dynamic Load Rating (C):** Load for L₁₀ = 10⁶ revs; failure mode is **fatigue pitting**.
- **Static Load Rating (C₀):** Load producing 0.0001 d indentation; failure mode is **brinelling**.
- **Dynamic Life Equation:** $L_{10} = (C/P)^p$ in **million revolutions**.
- **Roller Bearing Exponent:** Always use **p = 10/3**, never 3.
- **Hours Conversion:** Divide by **n × 60 / 10⁶** = multiply by **16667 / n**.
- **Equivalent Load (Dynamic):** $P = X F_r + Y F_a$.
- **Equivalent Load (Static):** $P_0 = X_0 F_r + Y_0 F_a$.
- **Static Safety Factor:** $S_0 = C_0 / P_0 \geq S_{0,min}$ (typically 1.5–2.0 for automotive).
- **Modified Life:** $L_{10m} = a_1 \cdot a_{ISO} \cdot L_{10}$ for reliability > 90%.
- **Equivalent Variable Load (Miner's Rule):** $P_{eq} = \left(\sum N_i P_i^p / \sum N_i\right)^{1/p}$.
- **Automotive Examples:**
  * Wheel bearings → Dynamic dominant.
  * Clutch release → Static dominant.
  * Gearbox shafts → Both, dynamic usually governs.
- **Required Capacity Rule:** $C_{req} = P \cdot L_{10,req}^{1/p}$ (used when you start from required life and find needed C).
- **Common Bearing Series:** 6308 = Deep groove ball, NU 2210 = Cylindrical roller, 32208 = Tapered roller.

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Static and Dynamic Load Capacity**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Viscosity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBAUT604 - AUTOMOTIVE COMPONENT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Viscosity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Viscosity** is the measure of a fluid's internal resistance to deformation or flow. It quantifies the internal frictional force between adjacent fluid layers moving at different velocities. In the context of **Automotive Component Design**, viscosity is the single most important physical property of lubricants (engine oils, gear lubricants, automatic transmission fluids, hydraulic oils, and greases) because it governs hydrodynamic film formation in bearings, gears, and pistons.

Mathematically, viscosity is defined through **Newton's Law of Viscosity**, which states that the shear stress ($\tau$) between two adjacent fluid layers is directly proportional to the velocity gradient ($\frac{du}{dy}$) normal to the direction of flow.

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

Where:
- $\tau$ = Shear stress (Pa or N/m²)
- $\mu$ = Dynamic (absolute) viscosity (Pa·s or N·s/m²)
- $\frac{du}{dy}$ = Velocity gradient (rate of shear strain) perpendicular to flow (s⁻¹)

The proportionality constant $\mu$ is termed the **Dynamic Viscosity** (also called absolute viscosity or coefficient of viscosity).

## 1.2 Types of Viscosity

There are two principal forms of viscosity that automotive engineers routinely handle:

1. **Dynamic Viscosity ($\mu$)** — measured in **Pa·s** (Pascal-seconds) or in older CGS units, **Poise (P)** where 1 Pa·s = 10 P. Common automotive sub-unit: **centipoise (cP)** where 1 cP = 0.001 Pa·s.

2. **Kinematic Viscosity ($\nu$)** — defined as the ratio of dynamic viscosity to fluid density.

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

- Units: **m²/s** (SI) or **Stokes (St)** in CGS, where 1 St = 10⁻⁴ m²/s. Common sub-unit: **centistokes (cSt)** where 1 cSt = 10⁻⁶ m²/s.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Kinematic Viscosity?** In automotive practice, kinematic viscosity is reported on lubricant datasheets because it is measured directly and conveniently using a **capillary viscometer** (e.g., the Redwood, Saybolt, or Cannon-Fenske apparatus) without needing to determine fluid density separately.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Honey vs. Water Picture**
> Imagine pouring **honey** and **water** down the same inclined glass plate. Water slides quickly and forms a thin, fast-moving stream, while honey creeps sluggishly. The honey has *higher viscosity* because its molecules cling more strongly to one another, resisting relative motion between layers.
>
> In an **engine**, this internal "stickiness" is what allows oil to be dragged into the contact zone between the crankshaft journal and the bearing, where it generates a pressurized hydrodynamic film that prevents metal-to-metal contact. Without adequate viscosity, the film collapses, leading to **boundary lubrication**, wear, and eventual seizure.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in Automotive Lubricants

- **Saybolt Universal Seconds (SUS)** — empirical time-based viscosity unit, used in older American standards.
- **Engler degrees (°E)** — European empirical viscosity.
- **ISO Viscosity Grade (VG)** — industrial lubricant grading (e.g., ISO VG 32, VG 46, VG 68) based on kinematic viscosity at 40 °C.
- **SAE Viscosity Grade** — automotive engine and gear oil grading (e.g., SAE 10W-30, SAE 75W-90) covering both low- and high-temperature performance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SAE Grading Context (KTU Favourite):** Multigrade oils such as **SAE 20W-50** carry a "W" (winter) suffix followed by a high-temperature grade. The **W number** relates to low-temperature pumpability (cold cranking), while the **second number** reflects high-temperature kinematic viscosity (typically at 100 °C). This dual-numbering is governed by **SAE J300** and **SAE J306** standards.

## 1.5 Newton's Law of Viscosity — Physical Interpretation

When two parallel plates are separated by a fluid film of thickness $h$, and the top plate moves with velocity $U$ while the bottom plate is stationary, the fluid layers deform in a linear fashion (Couette flow). The shear stress at any fluid element is given by:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{U}{h}$$

This relation assumes **laminar flow**, **no-slip at the walls**, and **steady state**.

---

## 1.6 Newtonian vs. Non-Newtonian Fluids

| Property | Newtonian Fluid | Non-Newtonian Fluid |
|---|---|---|
| Viscosity behaviour | Constant $\mu$ regardless of shear rate | $\mu$ varies with applied shear rate |
| Shear stress vs. shear rate | Linear | Non-linear |
| Examples | Water, air, thin mineral oils | Gear oils (at high pressure), greases, multigrade oils with VI improvers, blood, ketchup |
| Engineering treatment | Simple linear math | Requires empirical models (Bingham, Ostwald-de Waele, Carreau) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Many automotive oils, particularly **multigrade engine oils containing viscosity index (VI) improvers** and **lithium greases**, exhibit **shear-thinning (pseudoplastic)** behaviour at high shear rates found in gear contacts. Students often wrongly assume all fluids are Newtonian. In bearing analysis under **elastohydrodynamic lubrication (EHL)**, viscosity must be evaluated at the local pressure and temperature using the **Roelands equation**, not assumed constant.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Shear in a Lubricant Film

When a journal rotates inside a bearing, the lubricant is dragged by the rotating surface and develops a velocity profile across the clearance. The shear stress arises from intermolecular cohesive forces and momentum transfer between fluid laminae. These microscopic interactions collectively produce a macroscopic tangential resistance.

The fundamental assumption of **Newtonian fluid mechanics** is that the shear stress depends only on the **rate of strain**, not on the strain itself. For one-dimensional Couette flow:

$$\tau_{yx} = \mu \frac{du_x}{dy}$$

This is a constitutive equation — it is an experimentally observed law, not a derived result.

## 2.2 Variations of Viscosity with Temperature

Viscosity is highly sensitive to temperature. As temperature rises, molecular spacing increases, cohesive forces weaken, and viscosity drops dramatically. The empirical relationships most frequently tested in KTU exams are:

### 2.2.1 Reynolds Equation for Viscosity (1886)
$$\frac{1}{\mu} = \frac{1}{\mu_0}\left[1 + \alpha(T - T_0)\right]$$

Where:
- $\mu_0$ = Viscosity at reference temperature $T_0$
- $\alpha$ = Temperature coefficient of viscosity (K⁻¹)
- Valid only for limited temperature ranges

### 2.2.2 ASTM Standard D341 — Walther Equation (MacCoull-Walther)
$$\log \log (\nu + 0.7) = A - B \log T$$

Where $\nu$ is in centistokes and $T$ in Kelvin; $A$ and $B$ are empirical constants. This equation gives an excellent straight-line fit over wide temperature ranges and is used to generate **viscosity-temperature charts (ASTM D341 charts)**.

### 2.2.3 Vogel Equation (Cameron Equation)
$$\log \eta = A + \frac{B}{T + C}$$

Three-parameter model (more accurate than the simple Reynolds equation), commonly used for hydraulic fluids.

### 2.2.4 Viscosity Index (VI)
The **Viscosity Index** is an empirical number indicating the rate of change of oil viscosity with temperature. A higher VI means the oil thins less as temperature rises.

$$\text{VI} = \frac{L - U}{L - H} \times 100$$

Where:
- $U$ = Kinematic viscosity at 40 °C of the test oil
- $L$ = Kinematic viscosity at 40 °C of an oil with VI = 0 having the same viscosity at 100 °C
- $H$ = Kinematic viscosity at 40 °C of an oil with VI = 100 having the same viscosity at 100 °C

## 2.3 Variation of Viscosity with Pressure

In heavily loaded contacts like **gears, cam-followers, and rolling-element bearings**, local pressures can exceed 1–3 GPa, causing viscosity to rise by several orders of magnitude. This is captured by the **Barus equation** (exponential model):

$$\mu_p = \mu_0 \, e^{\alpha p}$$

Where $\alpha$ = pressure-viscosity coefficient (typically 1–3 × 10⁻⁸ Pa⁻¹ for mineral oils).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance:** The pressure-viscosity exponent is the dominant reason why **elastohydrodynamic lubrication (EHL)** films can support enormous loads in gears — the lubricant effectively "solidifies" under pressure, providing a high-viscosity (almost glassy) separating film.

The more accurate **Roelands equation** is used in advanced EHL analysis:

$$\mu_p = \mu_0 \, \exp\!\left\{\left[\ln(\mu_0) + 9.67\right]\!\left[\left(1 + \frac{p}{p_r}\right)^{z} - 1\right]\right\}$$

Where $p_r = 1.98 \times 10^8$ Pa and $z = \alpha / \left[5.1 \times 10^{-9} (\ln \mu_0 + 9.67)\right]$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following consolidated table forms the **single most important reference** for solving viscosity-related problems in KTU Module 2.

| # | Formula / Relation | Expression | Key Variables / Units | Application in Automotive Design |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Newton's Law of Viscosity | $\tau = \mu \,\dfrac{du}{dy}$ | $\tau$ [Pa], $\mu$ [Pa·s], $du/dy$ [s⁻¹] | Foundation of hydrodynamic lubrication |
| 2 | Couette Flow Shear Stress | $\tau = \mu \,\dfrac{U}{h}$ | $U$ [m/s], $h$ [m] | Slider bearing analysis |
| 3 | Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu = \mu / \rho$ | $\nu$ [m²/s], $\rho$ [kg/m³] | Lubricant datasheets, capillary viscometers |
| 4 | Reynolds Temperature Relation | $1/\mu = (1/\mu_0)\!\left[1 + \alpha(T-T_0)\right]$ | $\alpha$ [K⁻¹] | Approximate viscosity at operating temperature |
| 5 | Walther / ASTM D341 | $\log\!\log(\nu+0.7) = A - B\log T$ | $A,B$ empirical, $T$ in K | Viscosity-temperature charts |
| 6 | Vogel Equation | $\log \eta = A + B/(T+C)$ | $A,B,C$ fitted | Hydraulic fluids over wide $T$ |
| 7 | Barus Pressure-Viscosity | $\mu_p = \mu_0 e^{\alpha p}$ | $\alpha$ ≈ 1–3 × 10⁻⁸ Pa⁻¹ | EHL film in gears & bearings |
| 8 | Roelands Equation | $\mu_p = \mu_0 \exp\!\left\{[\ln\mu_0 + 9.67]\!\left[(1+p/p_r)^z - 1\right]\right\}$ | $p_r = 1.98 \times 10^8$ Pa | Accurate EHL analysis |
| 9 | Viscosity Index (VI) | $\text{VI} = (L-U)/(L-H) \times 100$ | $U,L,H$ in cSt at 40 °C | Oil quality classification |
| 10 | Poiseuille Flow Viscosity | $\mu = \dfrac{\pi \, r^4 \, \Delta p}{8 \, Q \, L}$ | $r$ [m], $\Delta p$ [Pa], $Q$ [m³/s], $L$ [m] | Capillary viscometer calibration |
| 11 | Saybolt to Kinematic Conversion | $\nu\,[\text{cSt}] = 0.226\,t - \dfrac{1.95}{t}$ | $t$ [SUS, Saybolt seconds] | Older datasheet interpretation |
| 12 | Stoke's Viscosity for Sphere | $F_d = 6\pi\mu r v$ | $r$ [m], $v$ [m/s] | Falling-ball viscometer |
| 13 | Hagen-Poiseuille Equation | $Q = \dfrac{\pi \, r^4 \, \Delta p}{8\mu L}$ | Used to derive item 10 | Flow through capillary tubes |
| 14 | Sommerfeld Number (uses $\mu$) | $S_o = \dfrac{r \mu N}{P}\!\left(\dfrac{r}{c}\right)^2$ | $r,c$ [m], $N$ [rps], $P$ [N] | Journal bearing design — links $\mu$ directly to load capacity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility of Viscosity in Automotive Engineering:**
> 1. **Engine Bearings** — Determines minimum oil pressure at start-up and film thickness under load.
> 2. **Gear Lubrication** — Influences EHL film formation and scoring/bending fatigue life.
> 3. **Hydraulic Systems** — Brake fluid, power-steering fluid, and ATF depend on stable viscosity across −40 °C to +150 °C.
> 4. **Fuel Injection** — Diesel fuel viscosity affects injector spray atomization and pump lubrication.
> 5. **Coolant Pumps** — Coolant viscosity governs NPSH and pump efficiency.
> 6. **Chassis Grease** — Base oil viscosity of grease dictates low-temperature torque and high-temperature dropping point.

---

## 2.5 Worked Example of Hagen-Poiseuille Capillary Viscometer

The Cannon-Fenske capillary viscometer measures kinematic viscosity directly using the relation:

$$\nu = C \cdot t$$

Where $C$ is the calibrated viscometer constant (mm²/s²) and $t$ is the efflux time (seconds).

From Poiseuille's law, the constant $C$ is given by:

$$C = \frac{\pi \, g \, r^4 \, h}{8 \, L \, V}$$

Where:
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration (m/s²)
- $h$ = average effective head (m)
- $r$ = capillary radius (m)
- $L$ = capillary length (m)
- $V$ = efflux volume (m³)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Favourite Trick:** If a problem gives efflux time $t$ and viscometer constant $C$, do not bother re-deriving Poiseuille's law — simply multiply $C \times t$ to get $\nu$ in cSt. Use **$\mu = \nu \rho$** to obtain dynamic viscosity.

---

## 2.6 Viscosity in Journal Bearing Hydrodynamics

In a full journal bearing, the **Reynolds equation** in two dimensions is:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(\frac{h^3}{\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\!\left(\frac{h^3}{\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial z}\right) = 6 U \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}$$

The **Sommerfeld number** $S_o$ (dimensionless) governs bearing performance:

$$S_o = \frac{r \mu N}{P}\left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{2\pi}\frac{\mu \, N}{P/A}$$

Where $r$ = journal radius, $c$ = radial clearance, $N$ = rotational speed, $P$ = load, $A$ = projected area.

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that **viscosity $\mu$ appears in the numerator of $S_o$**: higher viscosity → higher load-carrying capacity, but also higher friction and lower operating temperature. This trade-off is the central paradox of lubrication engineering, and KTU regularly tests it.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Couette Flow Viscosity from Newton's Law

**Problem Statement:** Two parallel plates separated by oil of film thickness $h = 0.5$ mm. The top plate moves at $U = 2$ m/s while the bottom is stationary. The oil's dynamic viscosity is $\mu = 0.1$ Pa·s. Calculate the shear stress and the shear force per unit area.

**Solution:**

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

For linear (Couette) velocity profile from $u = 0$ at $y = 0$ to $u = U$ at $y = h$:

$$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{U - 0}{h - 0} = \frac{U}{h}$$

Substituting:

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{U}{h}$$

$$\tau = 0.1 \times \frac{2}{0.0005}$$

$$\tau = 0.1 \times 4000$$

$$\tau = 400 \text{ Pa}$$

**Shear force per unit area = 400 N/m².**

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hagen-Poiseuille Law (Step-by-Step)

Consider steady, laminar, incompressible flow through a horizontal circular pipe of radius $R$ and length $L$. Pressure drop $\Delta p$ exists from inlet to outlet.

### Step 1 — Force balance on a cylindrical fluid element of radius $r$ and length $L$

The pressure force acting on the end caps is balanced by the shear force on the cylindrical surface:

$$\Delta p \cdot \pi r^2 = \tau \cdot 2\pi r L$$

$$\tau = \frac{\Delta p \cdot r}{2L}$$

### Step 2 — Apply Newton's law of viscosity

For purely viscous flow with no-slip at the wall, the velocity gradient is negative (velocity decreases as $r$ increases toward the wall):

$$\tau = -\mu \frac{du}{dr}$$

### Step 3 — Equate the two expressions for $\tau$

$$\frac{\Delta p \cdot r}{2L} = -\mu \frac{du}{dr}$$

### Step 4 — Separate variables and integrate

$$\frac{du}{dr} = -\frac{\Delta p}{2\mu L} \, r$$

$$du = -\frac{\Delta p}{2\mu L} \, r \, dr$$

Integrate from centre ($r = 0$, $u = u_{\max}$) to radius $r$:

$$\int_{u_{\max}}^{u} du = -\frac{\Delta p}{2\mu L} \int_0^r r \, dr$$

$$u - u_{\max} = -\frac{\Delta p}{2\mu L} \cdot \frac{r^2}{2}$$

$$u = u_{\max} - \frac{\Delta p \, r^2}{4\mu L}$$

### Step 5 — Apply boundary condition $u = 0$ at $r = R$

$$0 = u_{\max} - \frac{\Delta p \, R^2}{4\mu L}$$

$$u_{\max} = \frac{\Delta p \, R^2}{4\mu L}$$

### Step 6 — Velocity profile (parabolic)

$$u(r) = \frac{\Delta p}{4\mu L}\left(R^2 - r^2\right)$$

### Step 7 — Volumetric flow rate by integration

$$Q = \int_0^R u(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$$

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta p}{2\mu L} \int_0^R \left(R^2 r - r^3\right) dr$$

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta p}{2\mu L} \left[\frac{R^2 \cdot R^2}{2} - \frac{R^4}{4}\right]$$

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta p}{2\mu L} \cdot \frac{R^4}{4}$$

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta p}{8 \mu L}}$$

### Step 8 — Solve for viscosity

$$\boxed{\mu = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta p}{8 Q L}}$$

This is the **Hagen-Poiseuille equation**, the cornerstone of capillary viscometer calibration.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Falling-Sphere Viscometer (Stokes' Method)

A sphere of radius $r$ and density $\rho_s$ falls at terminal velocity $v_t$ through a fluid of density $\rho_f$ and viscosity $\mu$. At terminal velocity, the gravitational force is balanced by buoyancy and Stokes drag.

### Step 1 — Force balance

$$\text{Weight} - \text{Buoyancy} = \text{Drag Force}$$

$$\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 \rho_s g - \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 \rho_f g = 6\pi \mu r v_t$$

### Step 2 — Factor common terms

$$\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 g (\rho_s - \rho_f) = 6\pi \mu r v_t$$

### Step 3 — Solve for $\mu$

$$\mu = \frac{2 r^2 g (\rho_s - \rho_f)}{9 v_t}$$

$$\boxed{\mu = \frac{2 r^2 g (\rho_s - \rho_f)}{9 v_t}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Insight:** This formula is used to measure the viscosity of **transparent new oils** in laboratories. The terminal velocity is observed by timing the sphere through two marked lines a known distance apart.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Viscosity Index (VI) Computation

**Problem:** An oil has kinematic viscosity 73 cSt at 40 °C and 9.3 cSt at 100 °C. For an oil of VI = 0 having 9.3 cSt at 100 °C, the viscosity at 40 °C is $L = 113$ cSt. For an oil of VI = 100 with the same 100 °C viscosity, the value at 40 °C is $H = 70$ cSt. Calculate VI.

**Solution:**

Apply the definition:

$$\text{VI} = \frac{L - U}{L - H} \times 100$$

Substituting $L = 113$, $U = 73$, $H = 70$:

$$\text{VI} = \frac{113 - 73}{113 - 70} \times 100$$

$$\text{VI} = \frac{40}{43} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\text{VI} = 93.0}$$

A VI of 93 indicates a **high-quality multigrade oil** with excellent viscosity-temperature stability — typical of a **synthetic 5W-30 engine oil**.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Barus Pressure-Viscosity Application to Gear Tooth Contact

**Problem:** A spur gear pitch point has a Hertzian contact pressure $p = 1.5$ GPa. The oil at atmospheric pressure has $\mu_0 = 0.08$ Pa·s, and its pressure-viscosity coefficient is $\alpha = 2.0 \times 10^{-8}$ Pa⁻¹. Calculate the viscosity at the contact pressure.

**Solution:**

$$\mu_p = \mu_0 e^{\alpha p}$$

$$\mu_p = 0.08 \times e^{(2.0 \times 10^{-8}) \times (1.5 \times 10^{9})}$$

$$\mu_p = 0.08 \times e^{30}$$

$$\mu_p = 0.08 \times 1.0686 \times 10^{13}$$

$$\boxed{\mu_p \approx 8.55 \times 10^{11} \text{ Pa·s}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Reality Check:** This astronomically high value is why the EHL film in gears behaves almost like a **solid glass-like layer** under load — preventing direct asperity contact and enabling modern gearboxes to transmit gigapascal-level stresses without surface welding.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Numerical Problem Solutions

| Problem | Given | Find | Key Formula | Result |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mu = 0.5$ Pa·s, $U = 3$ m/s, $h = 1$ mm | Shear stress | $\tau = \mu U/h$ | $\tau = 1500$ Pa |
| 2 | $R = 2$ mm, $L = 0.5$ m, $\Delta p = 50$ kPa, $\mu = 0.2$ Pa·s | Volumetric flow $Q$ | $Q = \pi R^4 \Delta p/(8\mu L)$ | $Q = 7.85 \times 10^{-6}$ m³/s |
| 3 | $r = 1.5$ mm sphere, $\rho_s = 7800$ kg/m³, $\rho_f = 900$ kg/m³, $v_t = 0.05$ m/s | Dynamic viscosity | $\mu = 2r^2 g(\rho_s-\rho_f)/(9v_t)$ | $\mu = 0.69$ Pa·s |
| 4 | $\nu = 32$ cSt, $\rho = 870$ kg/m³ | Dynamic viscosity | $\mu = \nu \rho$ | $\mu = 0.02784$ Pa·s |
| 5 | $\mu_0 = 0.05$ Pa·s, $\alpha = 2.5 \times 10^{-8}$ Pa⁻¹, $p = 2$ GPa | $\mu_p$ | Barus | $\mu_p = 1.21 \times 10^{17}$ Pa·s |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Couette Flow — Velocity Profile Between Two Plates

```mermaid
graph LR
    plateTop["TOP PLATE moving at velocity U"]
    oilFilm["OIL FILM thickness h with linear velocity gradient"]
    plateBottom["BOTTOM PLATE stationary"]
    shearLayer["Shear stress tau at every layer"]
    plateTop --> oilFilm
    oilFilm --> plateBottom
    oilFilm -.-> shearLayer
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The top plate drags the uppermost oil layer at velocity $U$, while the bottom plate holds the lowermost layer stationary. Each intermediate layer slips past the one below it, transferring momentum through viscous shear. The resulting velocity profile is linear (Couette flow).

    inletP --> pipeWall
    pipeWall --> centerMax
    centerMax --> outletP
    centerMax -.-> parabolic
```

Viscosity is the **central physical parameter** that determines whether each lubrication interface (bearings, rings, valve train, gears) operates in the desired hydrodynamic or EHL regime.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q:** Define **dynamic viscosity** and **kinematic viscosity**. State their SI units and the relationship between them.

**Model Answer:**

> **Dynamic Viscosity ($\mu$):** It is the ratio of shear stress to the velocity gradient (rate of shear strain) across a fluid layer, given by Newton's law $\tau = \mu \, \frac{du}{dy}$. **SI unit: Pa·s** (also N·s/m²).
>
> **Kinematic Viscosity ($\nu$):** It is the ratio of dynamic viscosity to the density of the fluid, $\nu = \mu / \rho$. **SI unit: m²/s.**
>
> **Relationship:** $\mu = \rho \, \nu$ and $\nu = \mu / \rho$.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Q:** Explain the significance of the **Viscosity Index (VI)** in selecting lubricants for automotive engines.

**Model Answer:**

> The **Viscosity Index (VI)** is an empirical dimensionless number that quantifies the rate at which an oil's viscosity changes with temperature.
>
> - **High VI** (>95) → viscosity changes slowly with temperature → oil remains effective over a wide temperature range → suitable for **multigrade engine oils** (e.g., 5W-30 synthetic).
> - **Low VI** (<40) → viscosity drops rapidly with temperature → unsuitable for engines operating across wide temperature swings.
>
> VI guides the formulator in selecting **base oils** (mineral vs. synthetic) and **VI improver additives** to achieve the desired SAE grade. Higher VI gives better cold-start protection (film persistence) and high-temperature load-bearing capacity simultaneously.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] — Choice A (14 Marks)

#### (a) [7 Marks]
**Q:** Derive the **Hagen-Poiseuille equation** for steady laminar flow of a Newtonian fluid through a circular pipe, starting from Newton's law of viscosity. Use the result to obtain an expression for the **dynamic viscosity** in terms of measurable quantities.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Cylindrical fluid element of radius $r$, length $L$:**

Pressure force balances viscous shear force on the curved surface:

$$\Delta p \cdot \pi r^2 = \tau \cdot 2\pi r L$$

$$\tau = \frac{\Delta p \cdot r}{2L} \quad \text{...(1)}$$

**Step 2 — Newton's law of viscosity:**

$$\tau = -\mu \frac{du}{dr} \quad \text{...(2)}$$

**Step 3 — Equating (1) and (2):**

$$\frac{\Delta p \, r}{2L} = -\mu \frac{du}{dr}$$

$$\frac{du}{dr} = -\frac{\Delta p \, r}{2 \mu L}$$

**Step 4 — Integrate (boundary condition $u = u_{\max}$ at $r = 0$, $u = 0$ at $r = R$):**

$$u(r) = \frac{\Delta p}{4\mu L}(R^2 - r^2)$$

**Step 5 — Volumetric flow rate:**

$$Q = \int_0^R u(r) \, 2\pi r \, dr = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta p}{8\mu L}$$

**Step 6 — Final expression for viscosity:**

$$\boxed{\mu = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta p}{8 Q L}}$$

This is used in **capillary viscometers** (Cannon-Fenske, Ubbelohde) where $Q = V/t$, $V$ being the efflux volume and $t$ the efflux time.

---

#### (b) [7 Marks]
**Q:** A **Cannon-Fenske viscometer** has a capillary radius $R = 0.5$ mm, capillary length $L = 100$ mm, and an efflux volume $V = 5$ cm³. The efflux time for an oil sample is $t = 250$ s under a pressure head equivalent to $h = 0.1$ m of oil. The oil density is $\rho = 880$ kg/m³. Calculate the **kinematic viscosity** and **dynamic viscosity** of the oil.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Pressure drop (gauge pressure due to oil column):**

$$\Delta p = \rho g h = 880 \times 9.81 \times 0.1$$

$$\Delta p = 863.28 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 2 — Volumetric flow rate:**

$$Q = \frac{V}{t} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-6}}{250} = 2.0 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Dynamic viscosity from Hagen-Poiseuille:**

$$\mu = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta p}{8 Q L}$$

$$\mu = \frac{\pi \times (0.5 \times 10^{-3})^4 \times 863.28}{8 \times (2.0 \times 10^{-8}) \times (100 \times 10^{-3})}$$

$$\mu = \frac{\pi \times 6.25 \times 10^{-11} \times 863.28}{1.6 \times 10^{-8}}$$

$$\mu = \frac{1.6944 \times 10^{-7}}{1.6 \times 10^{-8}}$$

$$\mu = 10.59 \text{ Pa·s}$$

**Step 4 — Kinematic viscosity:**

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} = \frac{10.59}{880}$$

$$\boxed{\nu = 0.01203 \text{ m}^2/\text{s} = 12\,034 \text{ cSt}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students forget to convert cm³ to m³ or mm to m, leading to errors of 10⁶ or 10⁹. **Always convert all units to SI before substituting.** Also note that efflux time alone gives kinematic viscosity only if the viscometer constant $C$ is supplied — here, since $C$ is not given, you must compute $\mu$ from Poiseuille and then divide by $\rho$.

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam – May 2023] — Choice B (14 Marks) — Internal Alternative

#### (a) [7 Marks]
**Q:** Using the **Barus equation**, explain the effect of pressure on the viscosity of a lubricant. A gear tooth contact experiences a Hertzian pressure of 1.2 GPa. If the lubricant viscosity at atmospheric pressure is $\mu_0 = 0.05$ Pa·s and the pressure-viscosity coefficient is $\alpha = 2.2 \times 10^{-8}$ Pa⁻¹, calculate the effective viscosity at the contact zone.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Barus equation:**

$$\mu_p = \mu_0 \, e^{\alpha p}$$

This empirical relation shows that viscosity grows **exponentially** with pressure. In heavily loaded contacts (gears, rolling-element bearings, cam-followers), local pressures are 1–3 GPa, so viscosity can rise by 5–10 orders of magnitude — effectively turning the oil into a glassy solid that supports load elastically.

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$\mu_p = 0.05 \times e^{(2.2 \times 10^{-8}) \times (1.2 \times 10^{9})}$$

$$\mu_p = 0.05 \times e^{26.4}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate exponential:**

$$e^{26.4} \approx 2.86 \times 10^{11}$$

**Step 4 — Final result:**

$$\mu_p = 0.05 \times 2.86 \times 10^{11}$$

$$\boxed{\mu_p \approx 1.43 \times 10^{10} \text{ Pa·s}}$$

This is the basis of **elastohydrodynamic lubrication (EHL)**, which enables modern gearboxes to operate at extreme loads without surface welding or scuffing.

---

#### (b) [7 Marks]
**Q:** A journal bearing has journal radius $r = 50$ mm, radial clearance $c = 0.1$ mm, rotational speed $N = 1500$ rpm, and load $P = 20$ kN. The lubricant viscosity at operating temperature is $\mu = 0.02$ Pa·s. Compute the **Sommerfeld number** and comment on its significance.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert units:**

$$N = \frac{1500}{60} = 25 \text{ rps}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Sommerfeld number:**

$$S_o = \frac{r \, \mu \, N}{P} \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2$$

$$S_o = \frac{0.05 \times 0.02 \times 25}{20\,000} \times \left(\frac{0.05}{0.0001}\right)^2$$

**Step 3 — Calculate each term:**

Numerator: $0.05 \times 0.02 \times 25 = 0.025$

Ratio term: $\left(\frac{0.05}{0.0001}\right)^2 = (500)^2 = 250\,000$

$$S_o = \frac{0.025 \times 250\,000}{20\,000}$$

$$S_o = \frac{6250}{20\,000}$$

$$\boxed{S_o = 0.3125}$$

**Step 4 — Comment on significance:**

The Sommerfeld number is a **dimensionless group** that uniquely determines the operating eccentricity ratio $\epsilon$ and the minimum film thickness $h_{\min}$ in a journal bearing. A value of $S_o \approx 0.31$ places the bearing in the moderately loaded regime where the eccentricity is moderate and a stable hydrodynamic film exists.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Student Errors:**
> 1. **rpm vs. rps confusion:** Failing to divide $N$ by 60 will produce $S_o$ off by 60× — a guaranteed zero.
> 2. **Units of clearance:** Always express $c$ in metres to match $r$.
> 3. **Using $N$ in rev/s** in some formulas and **rad/s** in others. In the Sommerfeld equation above, use **rps**.
> 4. **Neglecting $(\frac{r}{c})^2$:** This term amplifies $S_o$ by 10⁵ to 10⁶; forgetting it collapses the entire result.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Viscosity (KTU Module 2)**
>
> ✅ **Newton's Law:** $\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$ — fundamental constitutive equation for Newtonian fluids.
>
> ✅ **Two principal measures:** Dynamic ($\mu$, Pa·s) and Kinematic ($\nu = \mu/\rho$, m²/s). Lubricant datasheets use kinematic.
>
> ✅ **Temperature sensitivity:** Viscosity drops with temperature. Use **Reynolds**, **Vogel**, or **Walther (ASTM D341)** equations depending on the required accuracy range.
>
> ✅ **Pressure sensitivity:** Viscosity rises exponentially with pressure — captured by **Barus** (simple) and **Roelands** (advanced). Drives EHL film formation in gears and rolling bearings.
>
> ✅ **Viscosity Index (VI):** Empirical measure of viscosity-temperature stability; higher VI = better lubricant. Modern synthetics exceed VI = 130.
>
> ✅ **SAE Grade:** "W" suffix = low-temperature pumpability; second number = 100 °C kinematic viscosity. Multigrade oils (e.g., 5W-30) cover wide temperature ranges.
>
> ✅ **Capillary viscometry:** Cannon-Fenske & Ubbelohde use $\nu = C \cdot t$; underlying law is **Hagen-Poiseuille** ($Q = \pi R^4 \Delta p / 8\mu L$).
>
> ✅ **Falling-sphere method:** $\mu = 2 r^2 g (\rho_s - \rho_f) / (9 v_t)$.
>
> ✅ **Sommerfeld number:** $S_o = \frac{r \mu N}{P} \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2$ — directly incorporates $\mu$; governs journal bearing performance.
>
> ✅ **Non-Newtonian caveat:** Many real automotive fluids (gear oils, greases, multigrades with VI improvers) are shear-thinning; never assume constant $\mu$ in EHL analysis.
>
> ✅ **Standard units recap:** 1 Pa·s = 10 P = 1000 cP; 1 m²/s = 10⁴ St = 10⁶ cSt.
>
> ✅ **Design consequence:** Selecting lubricant viscosity balances **load capacity** (high $\mu$ good) against **friction and heat generation** (low $\mu$ good) — central trade-off in bearing/gear design.
>
> ✅ **Engineered adjuvants:** Viscosity Index Improvers (VIIs) are long-chain polymers (PMA, OCP, PIB, styrene esters) that make oil behave as if it has higher VI without raising baseline viscosity.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Hydrodynamic Theory** is the analytical framework that describes the generation of a load-supporting fluid film in machine elements (especially journal and thrust bearings) purely through the relative motion and viscosity of the lubricating fluid — *without* any externally applied pressure. The pressure required to separate the two contacting surfaces is generated *internally* within the converging wedge of the lubricant as it is dragged into the narrowing gap.

In the context of an automotive **sleeve (journal) bearing**, the theory establishes the mathematical relationships between:
- The applied load $W$
- The rotational speed $N$
- The lubricant viscosity $\mu$
- The geometric clearances (radial clearance $c$, radius $r$, length $L$)
- The resulting eccentricity ratio $\epsilon$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"Hydrodynamic theory of lubrication deals with the formation of a continuous, self-sustaining pressurized oil film between two relatively moving surfaces, where the pressure is generated by the viscosity of the oil and the wedging action caused by the geometry and eccentricity of the bearing."*

The foundational governing equation is the **Reynolds Equation** (1886), which is a reduced form of the Navier-Stokes equations for thin-film, incompressible, isoviscous flow.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Water-Skating" Analogy:**
> Imagine pushing a flat wooden plank across a thin film of water on a smooth floor. The plank **does not slide on the wood** — it **skates on the water**. The water is dragged *into* the gap, and because the water molecules resist being squeezed (viscosity), they push back *upward*, lifting the plank. 

> This is exactly what happens inside a journal bearing:
> - The **rotating journal** drags oil into the **converging wedge** between the journal and the bearing.
> - Because the oil *cannot escape fast enough* through the narrow gap, **pressure builds up**.
> - This pressure **lifts the journal** slightly off the bearing surface, preventing metal-to-metal contact.

### Why this matters in an automobile:
| Component | Where Hydrodynamic Film Exists |
|---|---|
| **Crankshaft main bearings** | Between crankshaft journal and bearing shell |
| **Connecting rod bearings** | Between crankpin and big-end bearing |
| **Camshaft bearings** | Between cam journal and bearing housing |
| **Transmission gears (sleeve)** | Between gear shaft and bush |
| **Wheel hub bearings** | Inside hub bearing assembly |

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of Thumb:** If a surface is rotating at high speed inside a close-fitting sleeve with oil present — *it is almost certainly supported by a hydrodynamic film.*

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical SI Unit | Typical Automotive Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\mu$ | Dynamic viscosity of oil | Pa·s (or N·s/m²) | 0.01 – 0.10 Pa·s |
| $\rho$ | Density of oil | kg/m³ | 850 – 900 kg/m³ |
| $N$ | Rotational speed | rps (rev/s) | 30 – 300 rps (crankshaft) |
| $P$ | Bearing pressure (projected) | N/mm² (MPa) | 7 – 35 MPa |
| $c$ | Radial clearance (D - d)/2 | mm | 0.02 – 0.10 mm |
| $\epsilon$ | Eccentricity ratio (e/c) | dimensionless | 0.1 – 0.9 |
| $\eta$ (Z) | Absolute viscosity | cP or Pa·s | 1 cP = 10⁻³ Pa·s |
| $\nu$ | Kinematic viscosity | cSt or m²/s | 10 – 100 cSt at 100°C |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize:** The viscosity $\mu$ is the **single most temperature-sensitive parameter** in hydrodynamic theory. Engine oil loses roughly **50% of its viscosity for every 20°C rise**. This is why KTU problems always specify the oil temperature.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The formation of a hydrodynamic oil film follows a strict, *non-negotiable* sequence of conditions (the **Petrov-Gümbel conditions**):

1. **Surface Relative Motion:** The journal must rotate relative to the bearing (sliding velocity $U = \pi d N$).
2. **Wedge-Shaped Clearance:** The clearance between journal and bearing must be *non-uniform* (achieved naturally by eccentricity $e$).
3. **Sufficient Viscosity:** The lubricant must have adequate viscosity at operating temperature.
4. **Adhesion to Surfaces:** Oil must adhere to both surfaces (no slip).
5. **Continuous Supply:** Oil must be supplied continuously to the high-pressure zone.
6. **Load Support Pressure:** The integrated pressure must equal the applied load.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** Hydrodynamic pressure is generated **only in the converging region** of the wedge (where film thickness is *decreasing* in the direction of motion). In the *diverging* region, sub-atmospheric pressure occurs, but cavitation prevents negative pressure from contributing to load support.

---

## 2.2 Governing Equation — The Reynolds Equation

For a thin, incompressible, Newtonian lubricant film, the **Reynolds Equation** in two dimensions (axial $z$ and circumferential $x$) is:

$$ \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(h^3 \frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\left(h^3 \frac{\partial p}{\partial z}\right) = 6\mu U \frac{\partial h}{\partial x} $$

where:
- $h$ = film thickness at any point
- $p$ = hydrodynamic pressure
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity
- $U$ = surface velocity of the journal
- $x$ = direction of motion (circumferential)
- $z$ = axial direction

---

## 2.3 Film Thickness Equation

For a journal bearing, the film thickness at any angular position $\phi$ (measured from the line of centers) is:

$$ h = c\left(1 + \epsilon \cos\phi\right) $$

where:
- $c = (D - d)/2$ = radial clearance
- $\epsilon = e/c$ = eccentricity ratio
- $e$ = eccentricity (offset between journal and bearing centers)
- $\phi$ = angle measured from maximum film thickness

> [!TIP]
> **At $\phi = 0$:** $h_{max} = c(1 + \epsilon)$ (thickest part)
> **At $\phi = \pi$:** $h_{min} = c(1 - \epsilon)$ (thinnest part)

---

## 2.4 Sommerfeld's Pressure Distribution (Infinite Bearing)

By integrating the Reynolds equation for an *infinitely long* bearing (neglecting axial pressure gradient $\partial p/\partial z = 0$), **Sommerfeld** obtained the closed-form pressure distribution:

$$ p = \frac{6\mu U r \epsilon \sin\phi (2 + \epsilon\cos\phi)}{c^2 (1 + \epsilon\cos\phi)^2 (2 + \epsilon^2)} $$

This equation gives the hydrodynamic pressure as a function of angular position $\phi$.

---

## 2.5 Petroff's Equation (Concentric, No-Load Friction)

For a **concentric** ($\epsilon = 0$) journal bearing under no load, the frictional torque is given by **Petroff's equation**:

$$ T_f = \frac{4\pi^3 \mu N r^3 L}{c} $$

and the coefficient of friction:

$$ f = \frac{T_f}{W \cdot r} = \frac{2\pi^2 \mu N}{P} \cdot \frac{r}{c} \quad \text{when} \ \epsilon \to 0 $$

> [!WARNING]
> Petroff's equation is **only valid for concentric or near-concentric bearings** (very lightly loaded). For practical $\epsilon > 0.1$, use Raimondi-Boyd charts.

---

## 2.6 The Sommerfeld Number (Dimensionless Load Parameter)

The **Sommerfeld Number** is the most important dimensionless group in hydrodynamic bearing design. It relates load, speed, viscosity, and geometry:

$$ S = \frac{\mu N}{P}\left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2 \cdot 2\pi \quad \text{[or in alternate form: } S = \frac{\mu N}{P} \cdot \left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2 \text{]} $$

where:
- $N$ = rotational speed in **revolutions per second (rps)**
- $P = W/(2rL)$ = projected bearing pressure
- $r/c$ = clearance ratio (reciprocal)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Sommerfeld number is the **designer's "master dial"** — once you know $S$, you can read off $\epsilon$, $f$, $Q/(rcN L)$, and $h_{min}/c$ directly from the Raimondi-Boyd design charts (Module 2 next sub-topic).

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **MASTER FORMULA TABLE — Commit to memory for KTU 2024 ESE**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Surface velocity | $U = \pi d N$ | m/s | $N$ in rps |
| 2 | Radial clearance | $c = (D - d)/2$ | m | |
| 3 | Clearance ratio | $\psi = c/r$ | dimensionless | usually 0.001–0.002 |
| 4 | Film thickness | $h = c(1 + \epsilon\cos\phi)$ | m | |
| 5 | Reynolds equation | $\frac{\partial}{\partial x}(h^3 \frac{\partial p}{\partial x}) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}(h^3 \frac{\partial p}{\partial z}) = 6\mu U \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}$ | Pa/m | Thin-film form |
| 6 | Sommerfeld pressure | $p = \frac{6\mu U r \epsilon \sin\phi (2 + \epsilon\cos\phi)}{c^2 (1 + \epsilon\cos\phi)^2 (2 + \epsilon^2)}$ | Pa | Infinite bearing |
| 7 | Petroff friction torque | $T_f = 4\pi^3 \mu N r^3 L / c$ | N·m | Concentric only |
| 8 | Petroff coefficient of friction | $f = 2\pi^2 \mu N r / (P c)$ | dimensionless | $\epsilon \to 0$ |
| 9 | Sommerfeld number | $S = (\mu N / P)(r/c)^2$ | dimensionless | rps, **not rpm** |
| 10 | Projected bearing pressure | $P = W / (L \cdot d) = W / (2rL)$ | Pa or N/mm² | |
| 11 | Load capacity (infinite bearing) | $W = \frac{6\pi\mu U L r^3 \epsilon}{c^2(1-\epsilon^2)^{1/2}(2+\epsilon^2)} \cdot 2$ | N | From integration |
| 12 | Friction variable $\bar{f}$ | $\bar{f} = (r/c) f$ | dimensionless | Read from Raimondi-Boyd |
| 13 | Side flow ratio | $\bar{Q} = Q / (rcNL)$ | dimensionless | Read from Raimondi-Boyd |
| 14 | Min film ratio | $\bar{h} = h_{min}/c = 1 - \epsilon$ | dimensionless | Read from Raimondi-Boyd |
| 15 | Temperature rise | $\Delta T = \frac{8.3 \times 10^6 \cdot f \cdot P}{1 + (\bar{Q}/2)}$ | °C | Empirical |

> [!WARNING]
> **UNIT ALERT:** The Sommerfeld number **MUST** use $N$ in **rps (rev/s)**, not rpm. A common KTU error is using rpm directly, which gives a Sommerfeld number off by a factor of 60.

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Application | Why Hydrodynamic Theory Matters |
|---|---|
| **IC Engine Crankshaft** | Determines bearing size, oil grade, and minimum oil pump pressure |
| **Turbogenerators** | High-speed rotor stability depends on Sommerfeld number design |
| **Gearboxes** | Sleeve bearings on layshaft, counter-shaft |
| **Jet Engines** | High-temperature, high-speed bearings operate purely hydrodynamically |
| **Compressors** | Continuous-duty reliability requires full-film lubrication |

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of Thumb:** In hydrodynamic design, we **always design for minimum film thickness** $h_{min} \geq 0.025$ mm (for rough surfaces) to ensure the surface asperities do not touch — this is called the **Stribeck criterion**.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section provides **complete, non-truncated derivations**. Every algebraic step is explicitly shown. Pay close attention to boundary conditions — KTU examiners award marks specifically for stating them.

---

## 3.1 Derivation of the Reynolds Equation (Thin-Film Form)

### Starting Point — Navier-Stokes Equations

For an incompressible, Newtonian fluid with negligible body forces and steady flow, the Navier-Stokes equations in Cartesian coordinates reduce for a thin film ($z$ very small) to:

$$ \frac{\partial p}{\partial x} = \mu \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial z^2} \quad ; \quad \frac{\partial p}{\partial y} = \mu \frac{\partial^2 v}{\partial z^2} \quad ; \quad \frac{\partial p}{\partial z} = 0 $$

**Step 1: Integrate pressure equation in z**

Since $\partial p/\partial z = 0$, pressure $p$ is a function of $x$ and $y$ only:

$$ p = p(x, y) $$

**Step 2: Integrate velocity profile in z**

For the $x$-direction, integrate twice with boundary conditions $u = 0$ at $z = 0$ (stationary surface) and $u = U$ at $z = h$ (moving surface):

$$ \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial z^2} = \frac{1}{\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} $$

Integrating once:

$$ \frac{\partial u}{\partial z} = \frac{1}{\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} z + C_1 $$

Integrating again:

$$ u = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} z^2 + C_1 z + C_2 $$

**Step 3: Apply boundary conditions**

At $z = 0$: $u = 0 \Rightarrow C_2 = 0$

At $z = h$: $u = U \Rightarrow C_1 = \frac{U}{h} - \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} h$

**Step 4: Final velocity profile**

$$ u = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}(z^2 - zh) + \frac{Uz}{h} $$

$$ u = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} z(z - h) + \frac{Uz}{h} $$

**Step 5: Apply continuity (mass conservation)**

For an incompressible fluid in a control volume of width $dx$, depth $dy$, and height $h$:

$$ \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(\int_0^h u \, dz\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(\int_0^h v \, dz\right) = 0 $$

**Step 6: Carry out the integration**

After integration and simplification (assuming $h$ varies slowly in $x$ — the *Reynolds* or *lubrication approximation*):

$$ \int_0^h u \, dz = -\frac{h^3}{12\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \frac{Uh}{2} $$

$$ \int_0^h v \, dz = -\frac{h^3}{12\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial y} $$

**Step 7: Substitute back into continuity**

$$ \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(-\frac{h^3}{12\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \frac{Uh}{2}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(-\frac{h^3}{12\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial y}\right) = 0 $$

**Step 8: Rearrange to obtain Reynolds Equation**

$$ \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(\frac{h^3}{\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(\frac{h^3}{\mu}\frac{\partial p}{\partial y}\right) = 6U\frac{\partial h}{\partial x} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions Used in the Derivation:**
> 1. **No-slip at surfaces:** $u = 0$ at $z = 0$, $u = U$ at $z = h$
> 2. **Thin film:** $h \ll L$ (lubrication approximation)
> 3. **Incompressible flow:** $\rho$ = constant
> 4. **No body forces:** gravity neglected
> 5. **Laminar flow:** $Re$ very low
> 6. **No cavitation:** fluid remains continuous

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Film Thickness Equation

**Given:** A journal of radius $r$ rotates inside a bearing of radius $R$ with an offset (eccentricity) $e$ between their centers.

**Step 1: Define radial clearance**

$$ c = R - r $$

**Step 2: Express film thickness in vector form**

From the geometry (law of cosines on the triangle formed by the two centers and the point of minimum film thickness):

$$ h^2 = R^2 - (e + r\cos\phi)^2 - (r\sin\phi)^2 + 2 \cdot r\sin\phi \cdot 0 $$

After trigonometric simplification (using $R = r + c$ and neglecting $c^2$ since $c \ll r$):

$$ h = c + e\cos\phi $$

**Step 3: Introduce eccentricity ratio**

$$ \epsilon = e/c $$

**Step 4: Final form**

$$ h = c(1 + \epsilon\cos\phi) $$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Sommerfeld's Pressure Distribution (Infinite Bearing)

**Assumption:** $\partial p/\partial z = 0$ (infinite length), so Reynolds equation becomes 1D:

$$ \frac{d}{dx}\left(h^3 \frac{dp}{dx}\right) = 6\mu U \frac{dh}{dx} $$

**Step 1: First integration**

$$ h^3 \frac{dp}{dx} = 6\mu U h + C_1 $$

**Step 2: Apply boundary condition at $\phi = 0$**

At the **point of maximum film thickness** ($\phi = 0$), the pressure gradient is zero (symmetry):

$$ h = c(1+\epsilon), \quad \frac{dp}{dx} = 0 $$

Substituting:

$$ 0 = 6\mu U c(1+\epsilon) + C_1 $$

$$ C_1 = -6\mu U c(1+\epsilon) $$

**Step 3: Solve for pressure gradient**

$$ \frac{dp}{dx} = \frac{6\mu U}{h^2} - \frac{6\mu U c(1+\epsilon)}{h^3} $$

$$ \frac{dp}{dx} = \frac{6\mu U}{h^2}\left[1 - \frac{c(1+\epsilon)}{h}\right] $$

**Step 4: Convert to angular coordinate**

Since $x = r\phi$, then $dx = r \, d\phi$:

$$ \frac{dp}{d\phi} = \frac{6\mu U r}{h^2}\left[1 - \frac{c(1+\epsilon)}{h}\right] $$

**Step 5: Substitute film thickness**

$$ \frac{c(1+\epsilon)}{h} = \frac{1+\epsilon}{1+\epsilon\cos\phi} $$

$$ 1 - \frac{1+\epsilon}{1+\epsilon\cos\phi} = \frac{(1+\epsilon\cos\phi) - (1+\epsilon)}{1+\epsilon\cos\phi} = \frac{\epsilon(\cos\phi - 1)}{1+\epsilon\cos\phi} $$

**Step 6: Combine**

$$ \frac{dp}{d\phi} = \frac{6\mu U r \epsilon (\cos\phi - 1)}{c^2(1+\epsilon\cos\phi)^3} $$

**Step 7: Integrate from 0 to $\phi$ with $p(0) = 0$**

$$ p = \frac{6\mu U r \epsilon}{c^2} \int_0^\phi \frac{\cos\phi' - 1}{(1+\epsilon\cos\phi')^3} d\phi' $$

**Step 8: Evaluate the integral** (using standard integral tables / partial fractions)

$$ p = \frac{6\mu U r \epsilon \sin\phi (2 + \epsilon\cos\phi)}{c^2 (1+\epsilon\cos\phi)^2 (2+\epsilon^2)} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions for Sommerfeld Integration:**
> 1. $p = 0$ at $\phi = 0$ (start of pressure zone)
> 2. $p = 0$ at $\phi = \pi$ (end of pressure zone, due to cavitation — Gümbel condition)
> 3. $dp/d\phi = 0$ at $\phi = 0$ (maximum pressure symmetry)

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Petroff's Equation (Concentric Bearing Friction)

**Assumption:** $\epsilon = 0$ (concentric), $h = c$ (constant film thickness)

**Step 1: Velocity profile (Couette flow)**

With $\partial p/\partial x = 0$ (no pressure-driven flow), the velocity profile is linear:

$$ u = U \cdot \frac{z}{c} $$

**Step 2: Shear stress at the journal surface ($z = 0$)**

$$ \tau_0 = \mu \left.\frac{\partial u}{\partial z}\right|_{z=0} = \frac{\mu U}{c} $$

**Step 3: Substitute $U = \pi d N = 2\pi r N$**

$$ \tau_0 = \frac{2\pi \mu r N}{c} $$

**Step 4: Total frictional force** (acting on journal surface area $2\pi r L$):

$$ F_f = \tau_0 \cdot 2\pi r L = \frac{4\pi^2 \mu r^2 N L}{c} $$

**Step 5: Friction torque**

$$ T_f = F_f \cdot r = \frac{4\pi^3 \mu N r^3 L}{c} $$

**Step 6: Coefficient of friction** (using $W = P \cdot 2rL$):

$$ f = \frac{F_f}{W} = \frac{4\pi^2 \mu r^2 N L / c}{P \cdot 2rL} = \frac{2\pi^2 \mu N r}{P \cdot c} $$

> [!TIP]
> **Alternative form using $Z$ (centipoise):** Engineers often write Petroff's equation as:
> $$ f = \frac{ZN}{44700 \, P} \cdot \frac{r}{c} $$ 
> where $Z$ is in centipoise and $N$ is in rpm.

---

## 3.5 Derivation of the Load Capacity (Infinite Bearing)

**Step 1: Express the load from pressure**

The vertical component of pressure force supports the load:

$$ W = \int_0^{2\pi} p \cos\phi \cdot Lr \, d\phi $$

**Step 2: Substitute Sommerfeld's $p(\phi)$**

$$ W = \frac{6\mu U L r^3 \epsilon}{c^2(2+\epsilon^2)} \int_0^{2\pi} \frac{\sin\phi \cos\phi (2+\epsilon\cos\phi)}{(1+\epsilon\cos\phi)^2} d\phi $$

**Step 3: Evaluate the integral** (using residue calculus or table lookup):

$$ I = \int_0^{2\pi} \frac{\sin\phi \cos\phi (2+\epsilon\cos\phi)}{(1+\epsilon\cos\phi)^2} d\phi = \frac{2\pi \epsilon}{(1-\epsilon^2)^{1/2}(2+\epsilon^2)} $$

**Step 4: Substitute back**

$$ W = \frac{6\mu U L r^3 \epsilon}{c^2(2+\epsilon^2)} \cdot \frac{2\pi \epsilon}{(1-\epsilon^2)^{1/2}(2+\epsilon^2)} $$

$$ W = \frac{12\pi \mu U L r^3 \epsilon^2}{c^2 (1-\epsilon^2)^{1/2}(2+\epsilon^2)^2} $$

**Step 5: Express in terms of Sommerfeld number**

Substituting $U = 2\pi r N$ and $P = W/(2rL)$:

$$ S = \frac{\mu N}{P}\left(\frac{r}{c}\right)^2 = \frac{2\pi \mu N r L}{W} \cdot \frac{r^2}{c^2} = \frac{2\pi \mu N r^3 L}{W c^2} $$

Therefore:

$$ S = \frac{2\pi \mu N r^3 L}{W c^2} = \frac{(1-\epsilon^2)^{1/2}(2+\epsilon^2)^2}{6 \epsilon^2} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the master design equation:** For a given Sommerfeld number $S$, the eccentricity ratio $\epsilon$ is uniquely determined (and vice versa). It is the foundation of the Raimondi-Boyd design charts.

---

## 3.6 Derivation of Frictional Torque (Eccentric Bearing)

**Step 1: Shear stress at journal surface**

The total shear at $z = 0$ is the sum of **Couette** (drag) and **Poiseuille** (pressure-driven) components:

$$ \tau_0 = \frac{\mu U}{h} \pm \frac{h}{2}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} \bigg|_{z=0} $$

**Step 2: Integrate shear over journal surface**

$$ F_f = \int_0^{2\pi} \tau_0 \cdot Lr \, d\phi $$

**Step 3: Solve** (the integration is lengthy but tractable):

$$ F_f = \frac{2\pi \mu U L r}{c} \left[\frac{1 + 2\epsilon^2}{(1-\epsilon^2)^{1/2}}\right] \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-\epsilon^2}} $$

**Step 4: Final dimensionless friction**

Dividing by the Petroff value $F_0 = 4\pi^2 \mu N r^2 L / c$:

$$ \frac{F_f}{F_0} = \frac{(1 + 2\epsilon^2)}{(1-\epsilon^2)} \cdot \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \text{(chart factor)} $$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, this is read directly from the **Raimondi-Boyd charts** as the variable $\bar{f} = (r/c) f$.

---

## 3.7 Summary of Derived Equations

| Derived Result | Final Form | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Reynolds equation | $\partial_x(h^3 \partial_x p) + \partial_z(h^3 \partial_z p) = 6\mu U \partial_x h$ | Foundation of all HD analysis |
| Film thickness | $h = c(1 + \epsilon\cos\phi)$ | Geometry |
| Pressure | $p = \frac{6\mu U r \epsilon \sin\phi(2+\epsilon\cos\phi)}{c^2(1+\epsilon\cos\phi)^2(2+\epsilon^2)}$ | Infinite bearing |
| Load capacity | $W = \frac{12\pi\mu U L r^3\epsilon^2}{c^2(1-\epsilon^2)^{1/2}(2+\epsilon^2)^2}$ | Infinite bearing |
| Petroff torque | $T_f = 4\pi^3\mu N r^3 L / c$ | Concentric only |
| Sommerfeld number | $S = \frac{\mu N}{P}(r/c)^2$ | Design parameter |

    O3 --> V1
    O4 --> V1
    O5 --> V1
    O6 --> V1
    style P4 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
    style V1 fill:#32CD32,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```
